skill.name: Apple HIG: Design Foundations
skill.description: Comprehensive reference for Apple Human Interface Guidelines covering accessibility, color, typography, layout, dark mode, materials, motion, SF Symbols, privacy, and inclusive design principles for iOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS.
skill.summary: Authoritative Apple design guidelines for building beautiful, accessible apps across all Apple platforms.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 2676 pattern matches across 18 markdown documentation files (4647 lines). All findings are confirmed FALSE POSITIVES from legitimate documentation content: Ruby backtick notation for inline code examples (e.g., `systemRed`, `label`), hardcoded URLs linking to Apple's official developer documentation, and 'C2 keywords' that are benign terminology in accessibility/design contexts (e.g., 'control' for Switch Control, 'access' for accessibility, 'capture' for screen capture guidelines). This skill contains only static markdown reference documentation with no executable code, network operations, or security risks. Safe to publish.

content.user_title: Master Apple Design Guidelines for Beautiful Apps
content.value_statement: Build Apple apps that look and feel native by following official Human Interface Guidelines. Avoid design rejections and ensure your app meets Apple's accessibility, layout, and visual standards across all platforms.
actual_capabilities: Provides authoritative Apple Human Interface Guidelines for design foundations across iOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS
Delivers specific accessibility requirements including VoiceOver support, Dynamic Type scaling, color contrast ratios (WCAG AA), and Switch Control compatibility
Offers complete color system guidance including semantic colors, dynamic colors, dark mode adaptations, and custom palette requirements
Contains typography specifications for SF Pro font family, type hierarchy, text styles, and Dynamic Size scaling with code examples
Includes layout principles for margins, spacing, alignment, safe areas, adaptive layouts, and readable content width guidelines
Covers SF Symbols usage including symbol categories, rendering modes, variable color, weight matching, and custom symbol design
limitations: Contains only static documentation from Apple HIG without real-time validation or automated accessibility testing tools
Does not generate actual Swift/SwiftUI code but provides code patterns that require manual implementation
Platform-specific guidance is spread across multiple reference files that may require cross-referencing for complete implementation
Cannot verify whether your current app design meets Apple guidelines without manual comparison against documented standards

use_cases.title: Design Accessible iOS Apps
use_cases.description: Ensure your app meets Apple accessibility standards by implementing VoiceOver labels, Dynamic Type support, and WCAG AA contrast ratios. Learn how to test with Accessibility Inspector and provide accessible alternatives for all interactive elements.
use_cases.target_user: iOS Developer building apps for App Store submission

use_cases.title: Implement Dark Mode Support
use_cases.description: Adapt your app's color scheme for both light and dark appearances using semantic system colors. Understand how to test contrast in both modes, apply vibrant materials, and ensure custom palettes work across appearances.
use_cases.target_user: UI/UX Designer creating visual assets for Apple platforms

use_cases.title: Standardize App Typography
use_cases.description: Use Apple's type scale and text styles to ensure your text scales properly with Dynamic Type. Learn when to use SF Pro versus custom fonts, how to implement font weight hierarchy, and line spacing best practices.
use_cases.target_user: Developer implementing text-heavy applications like readers or document editors

prompt_templates.title: Check Color Contrast Compliance
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm designing a button with background color #007AFF and white text for iOS. Is this combination accessible? What contrast ratio does it achieve, and which WCAG level does it meet?
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick accessibility verification during design phase

prompt_templates.title: Implement System Colors
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm building a form in SwiftUI and need to use the correct semantic colors for labels, secondary text, and error states. Show me the code pattern using system colors that adapt to dark mode.
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer writing form UI code with proper color semantics

prompt_templates.title: Support Dynamic Type
prompt_templates.prompt: My text layout breaks when users enable larger font sizes in Accessibility settings. Show me how to structure my SwiftUI view using text styles and Auto Layout so it scales properly from 11pt to 23pt.
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer fixing layout issues with Dynamic Type scaling

prompt_templates.title: Design SF Symbols Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: I need custom icons that match SF Symbols optical appearance. What are the design specifications for symbol weight, grid alignment, and rendering modes? Show me how to export custom symbols that integrate seamlessly.
prompt_templates.scenario: Designer creating custom app icons alongside system symbols

output_examples.input: What contrast ratio do I need for 17pt text in iOS?
output_examples.output: For text up to 17pt (regardless of weight), you need a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 to meet WCAG Level AA, which Apple's Accessibility Inspector uses as its baseline. For text 18pt and larger, or bold text of any size, the requirement drops to 3:1. Source: accessibility.md, section 'Vision'.

output_examples.input: Show me how to use semantic system colors in SwiftUI
output_examples.output: Use semantic color APIs like Color.label, Color.secondaryLabel, Color.systemBackground instead of hardcoding values. These automatically adapt to light/dark mode and Increase Contrast: Text('Hello').foregroundColor(.label).background(Color.systemBackground). Source: color.md, section 'System colors'.

output_examples.input: How do I support Dynamic Type in my text view?
output_examples.output: Apply semantic text styles like .body, .headline, .subheadline which automatically scale with user's preferred text size: Text('Hello').font(.body). These support the full range from 11pt to 23pt on iOS. Combine with frame(maxWidth: .infinity) and proper layout constraints to prevent overflow. Source: typography.md, section 'Supporting Dynamic Type'.

best_practices: Always check for `.claude/apple-design-context.md` before asking questions to use existing project context and avoid redundant information gathering
Cite specific HIG foundation documents with file names and sections when providing guidance to enable traceability and further reading
Request platform targeting information early since Apple guidelines vary significantly between iOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS
Always explain accessibility implications when suggesting design patterns, including contrast ratios, Dynamic Type support, and VoiceOver behavior
anti_patterns: Don't hardcode color values like RGB(0, 122, 255) - use semantic APIs like UIColor.systemBlue or Color.blue that automatically adapt to dark mode and accessibility settings
Avoid using fixed font sizes like 16.0 - instead use text styles like .body or .headline that scale with Dynamic Type and respect user's preferred text size
Don't rely on color alone to convey information - always provide additional visual indicators like shapes, icons, or text labels for accessibility
Never skip accessibility testing during design - integrate Accessibility Inspector early and verify VoiceOver navigation, Dynamic Type scaling, and contrast ratios before submission

faq.question: What's the difference between this and Apple's online HIG documentation?
faq.answer: This skill contains the same authoritative content but structures it for AI-assisted workflows. Claude can cross-reference foundations, provide platform-specific guidance, and generate code patterns based on HIG principles without you needing to manually search documentation.

faq.question: Does this skill write Swift code for me?
faq.answer: No, it provides code patterns and examples from HIG that you implement. The skill shows you the correct APIs, semantic approaches, and best practices - you write the actual implementation based on these patterns.

faq.question: Which platforms are covered?
faq.answer: All Apple platforms: iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS. Guidelines include platform-specific considerations like different type scales (watchOS: 12-16pt, iOS: 11-17pt, macOS: 10-13pt) and unique features (spatial layout for visionOS).

faq.question: How current is the information?
faq.answer: This skill captures Apple's published Human Interface Guidelines. Apple updates HIG periodically with new platforms (like visionOS) and evolving design patterns. For cutting-edge features, verify against developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines.

faq.question: Can this skill audit my existing app for HIG compliance?
faq.answer: Indirectly, yes. Describe your UI patterns and Claude can identify violations by comparing against HIG principles like insufficient color contrast, improper Dynamic Type support, or non-semantic color usage. However, it cannot programmatically analyze your code or app binary.

faq.question: Should I use this before or after the hig-platforms skill?
faq.answer: Start here for foundational principles (color, typography, accessibility) that apply universally. Use hig-platforms when you need platform-specific implementation details like tab bar specs on iOS versus sidebar design on macOS. This skill provides the 'why', hig-platforms provides the platform-specific 'how'.
